full_response:
Let the apex (tip) of the cone be at the origin O and let the symmetry axis be the +z-axis.  
At height z the circular cross–section has radius that grows linearly from 0 (at z=0) to r (at z=h):

ρ(z)= r h z , where ρ ≡ √(x²+y²).

Denote by α the full opening angle of the cone surface, i.e. the angle between the two generators of the cone measured through the interior.  From simple right-triangle geometry at the rim (z=h, ρ=r)

tan(α/2)= r h  ⇔  α=2 arctan r h.     (1)

1.  Equation for the solid cone (including its boundary).

Using cylindrical (ρ,φ,z) or Cartesian (x,y,z) coordinates,

C≡{(x,y,z)∈ℝ³ | 0 ≤ z ≤ h   and   ρ ≤ r h z }             (2a)

or, substituting (1),

C≡{(x,y,z) | 0 ≤ z ≤ h   and   x²+y² ≤ z² tan²(α/2)}.      (2b)

Equation (2b) describes the entire finite three-dimensional cone, including its lateral surface (where the inequality becomes an equality) and its circular base (z=h).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Any point inside the cone makes an angle ≤α/2 with the +z-axis.

Pick an arbitrary point p=(x,y,z) belonging to C.  
Because p satisfies (2b),

x²+y² ≤ z² tan²(α/2).                                     (3)

Let θ be the angle between the vector  Op  and the +z-axis.  In spherical coordinates,

tanθ = ρ z = √(x²+y²) z.                                   (4)

Combining (3) and (4):

tanθ ≤ tan(α/2)  ⇒  θ ≤ α/2.                              (5)

Therefore every point of the solid cone lies on a ray from the origin whose angle with the +z-axis is at most α/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Converse: a point on such a ray lies inside the cone.

Conversely, take any ray emanating from O that forms an angle θ with the +z-axis where θ ≤ α/2.  
For every point on this ray with 0 ≤ z ≤ h we have

ρ = z tanθ ≤ z tan(α/2) = z (r/h),                        (6)

so x²+y² = ρ² ≤ z²(r/h)², i.e. inequality (2b) is satisfied.  
Hence the entire segment of such a ray between z=0 and z=h lies inside (or on the surface of) the solid cone, which completes the proof.

\boxed{C=\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}\;|\;0\le z\le h,\;x^{2}+y^{2}\le z^{2}\tan^{2}(\alpha/2)\},\quad
\forall p\in C:\ \angle(\overrightarrow{Op},+z) \le \alpha/2\;\text{and conversely}} 

Confidence: 86%
